Abram was very rich; he was also very heavy, as the Hebrew word indicates, because riches are a burden, and those who desire to be rich often load themselves with unnecessary worries. There is worry about acquiring riches, fear about keeping them, temptation in using them, guilt in misusing them, sorrow in losing them, and an accounting to give at last. Yet God, in his care for us, sometimes makes good men wealthy, and this is how God’s blessing made Abram rich without sorrow. Although it is difficult for a rich person to reach heaven, it may be possible in some cases. No, outward prosperity, if managed well, is an ornament to piety and provides an opportunity to do more good. Abram moved to Bethel. His altar was gone, so he could not offer sacrifice; but he called on the name of the Lord. You cannot find a living person without breath just as you cannot find someone who belongs to God without prayer.

Riches not only cause conflict and are often what people fight over, but they also stir up a spirit of contention by making people proud and greedy. 'Mine' and 'yours' are the main causes of trouble in the world. Poverty and labor, needs and wandering, could not separate Abram and Lot; but riches did. Bad servants often cause great problems for families and neighbors through their pride and anger, lying, slander, and gossip. What worsened the conflict was that the Canaanite and the Perizzite people lived in the land at that time. The disagreements among those who profess faith are a disgrace to religion and give enemies of the Lord reason to blaspheme. It is best to maintain peace so it is not broken; but if differences do arise, it is important to quickly put out the fire. Abram attempted to stop this conflict, even though he was older and more powerful. Abram showed himself to be calm and in control of his emotions, knowing how to turn away anger with gentle words. Those who want to keep peace must never respond to insults with insults. He had a humble spirit; he was willing to ask even someone less important than himself to make peace. People of God must always strive for peace. Abram’s plea for peace was very effective. Let people argue over unimportant things, but let us not quarrel, who know better and look forward to a better place. Those who profess faith should be especially careful to avoid conflict. Many claim to want peace but do nothing to achieve it; Abram did. When God asks us to be reconciled, we should ask one another to be reconciled as well. Although God had promised Abram this land for his descendants, he offered Lot an equal or better share, even though Lot did not have the same right; and he will not act unfairly toward a relative under the protection of God’s promise. It is noble to be willing to yield for peace’s sake.

After Abram offered Lot the choice, he immediately accepted it. Passion and selfishness make people rude. Lot looked at the fertility of the land; therefore, he did not doubt that he would certainly prosper in such a fertile area. But what happened? Those who choose relationships, careers, homes, or locations based on their physical desires, their eyes’ desires, or pride will not experience God's presence or blessing. They are often disappointed even in what they primarily seek. In all our choices, this principle should guide us: That which is best for our souls is best for us. Lot did not consider the wickedness of the inhabitants. The people of Sodom were openly sinful and disrespectful. This was the sin of Sodom: pride, plenty of food, and laziness. God often gives great abundance to great sinners. It has often been a hardship for good people to live among wicked neighbors; and it is more painful if they have brought this upon themselves through a poor choice.

Those who are best prepared for the visits of God’s grace are those whose spirits are calm and not troubled by passion. God will abundantly make up in spiritual peace what we lose when preserving neighborly peace. Even when our loved ones are separated from us, God is still with us. Also observe the promises with which God now comforted and enriched Abram. He assures him of two things: a good land and numerous descendants to enjoy it. The prospects seen by faith are richer and more beautiful than those we see around us. God told him to walk through the land, not to think about settling there, but to expect to be constantly moving toward a better Canaan. He built an altar as a sign of his thankfulness to God. When God meets us with gracious promises, he expects that we should respond with humble praise. In difficult circumstances, it is very helpful for true believers to meditate on the glorious inheritance that the Lord has promised them at the end.
